message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://www.isca-archive.org/interspeech_2025/lechler25_interspeech.pdf
**Title:** Crowdsourcing MUSHRA Tests in the Age of Generative Speech Technologies (Interspeech 2025)
**Found via:** engines-config + measurement search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll fetch the source and extract relevant claims.

input.prompt: Extract key findings about MUSHRA listening tests for evaluating speech/TTS quality, especially: crowdsourcing MUSHRA protocols, reliability/validity of crowdsourced vs lab MUSHRA, recommended sample sizes, anchors/references, screening of listeners, statistical methods, and any guidance relevant to evaluating generative/neural TTS. Provide direct verbatim quotes for each finding. Also note the authors, publication venue, and publication date/year.

content.content:
I apologize, but I cannot extract the requested information from the provided content. The text you've shared appears to be a PDF file encoded in binary format (containing compressed stream objects, font definitions, and image data), not readable text content.

To help you find key findings about MUSHRA listening tests for speech/TTS quality evaluation, I would need:

1. **Plain text or properly formatted document content** - Rather than PDF binary data
2. **Readable article excerpts** - With author names, publication venues, and dates clearly visible

**What I can suggest:**

- Try converting the PDF to plain text using a tool like `pdftotext`
- Copy and paste the actual article text directly
- Provide the DOI or title so I can help you structure the search differently

If you can share the readable text content of the paper(s) you're interested in, I'll be happy to extract the specific MUSHRA-related findings with proper attribution, verbatim quotes (under 125 characters), and methodological guidance relevant to generative/neural TTS evaluation.

[Binary content (application/pdf, 1.1MB) also saved to /Users/fred/.claude/projects/-Users-fred-Software-Engineering-HSC-Textbook-podcast-content/6b1689f5-87d0-40e5-9143-cb0867480812/tool-results/webfetch-1782029879507-muebwc.pdf]

content.text: The PDF was saved locally. Let me extract the text using pdftotext.

input.command: cd /tmp && cp "/Users/fred/.claude/projects/-Users-fred-Software-Engineering-HSC-Textbook-podcast-content/6b1689f5-87d0-40e5-9143-cb0867480812/tool-results/webfetch-1782029879507-muebwc.pdf" mushra.pdf && (pdftotext -layout mushra.pdf mushra.txt 2>&1 || echo "PDFTOTEXT_FAILED") && wc -l mushra.txt 2>/dev/null && echo "---FIRST PART---" && head -120 mushra.txt
input.description: Convert MUSHRA PDF to text and show header

content.text: I have the full content. This is a peer-reviewed Interspeech 2025 paper from Cisco Systems researchers. Let me extract the falsifiable claims relevant to the research question's angle (6) on measuring comprehensibility via MUSHRA listening tests.

claims.claim: Crowdsourced MUSHRA tests on Prolific reproduce expert-lab codec rankings and closely match absolute expert scores (Pearson 0.95, Spearman 0.91), making them a reliable, cheaper alternative to expert-listener tests for evaluating speech quality including generative codecs.
claims.quote: both Prolific and MTurk listeners produce the same overall ranking of codecs as Internal listeners (i.e., “ground truth”). However, Prolific’s scores more closely trace the Internal curve

claims.claim: Crowdsourcing platform choice introduces measurable bias: MTurk listeners show a ceiling effect and drift from expert absolute scores (Pearson 0.89-0.90), while Prolific aligns closely (0.95); reaching stable results required ~15 responses per item on Prolific vs ~25 on MTurk.
claims.quote: For Prolific, we observed stable results with approximately 15 responses for each test item, while MTurk required around 25 responses per test item.

claims.claim: For non-expert crowdsourced MUSHRA, the test should be simplified relative to the ITU-R BS.1534-3 expert protocol: at most 6 conditions in parallel (vs up to 12 for experts), shorter clips (~6.4 s avg vs the 10-12 s expert recommendation), and up to three rating blocks to limit fatigue.
claims.quote: In our experience, a maximum of 6 conditions should not be exceeded in a crowdsourced test.

claims.claim: Rigorous listener screening is essential for valid crowdsourced perceptual tests: pre-screen on task success rate >97% and >=100 completed tasks plus a digits-in-noise hearing test, and disqualify listeners who fail basic checks (anchor rated above hidden reference, or zero rating variance) on more than 20% of questions.
claims.quote: Listeners are disqualified entirely if they fail basic quality checks on more than 20% of the MUSHRA questions in a test block.

claims.claim: The MUSHRA anchor must introduce the same type of degradation as the systems under test; using a mismatched anchor (e.g. low-pass/bandwidth limitation against coding artifacts) made quality harder to judge and produced less consistent ratings for non-expert listeners.
claims.quote: participants found it harder to judge quality because the artifacts were of a different type (bandwidth limitation instead of coding distortion)

content.text:
Extracted 5 falsifiable claims from the Interspeech 2025 paper "Crowdsourcing MUSHRA Tests in the Age of Generative Speech Technologies" (Lechler, Moradi, Balic; Cisco Systems).

Source quality: primary (peer-reviewed conference paper, ISCA/Interspeech 2025 archive, with open-source code release). Published 2025-08-17.

The paper is most relevant to the research question's angle (6) — how to measure comprehensibility/quality at scale via MUSHRA listening tests. Key takeaways for the user's pipeline-evaluation decision:
- Crowdsourced MUSHRA can validly substitute for expensive expert-lab tests (Prolific Pearson 0.95 vs expert ground truth), which is directly useful for running A/B comparisons to decide whether a TTS fine-tune clears a "significant gain" bar.
- Practical protocol parameters: <=6 conditions per test, ~6.4 s clips, ~15 (Prolific) to ~25 (MTurk) responses per item, three-block max, and strict pre/post screening.
- Caveat for the user's use case: this paper evaluates speech codecs (quality preservation), not intelligibility at high playback speed, and tests use full natural-rate utterances — so the protocol is transferable but the specific stimuli/anchor choices would need adapting to a time-compression context. The paper's open-source tooling is at github.com/cisco/multilingual-speech-testing.

Local PDF/text saved at /tmp/mushra.pdf and /tmp/mushra.txt.
